_schema.description: Provenance-level claims: each entry is ONE source's assertion about ONE item. These are the evidence layer. seed-items.json aggregates them into per-item ground-truth verdicts. The RAG buckets and the checklist engine cite claim ids from here.

fields.category: documents | clothing | kitchen | food | medicines | electronics | bedding | toiletries | money
fields.context: conditions the verdict depends on: climate ([]), intake ([]), diet ([]), housing ([]); empty arrays = unconditional
fields.price: { inr, usd, note } — any of these may be null
fields.reasoning: why this verdict, per the source
fields.quote: verbatim supporting quote from the source
fields.confidence: high | medium | low — high only for the 2 adversarially-verified UIUC claims; everything else is extracted-but-unverified (medium for primary/consistent, low for single blog/forum or contested)

claims.weightNote: 3-5 L size chosen for luggage fit
claims.reasoning: Equivalent pressure cookers are expensive in the US.
claims.quote: Pressure Cooker (3-5 L)

claims.reasoning: Dry Indian spices worth carrying; cost more in small US towns.
claims.quote: Dry spices (turmeric, garam masala, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, asafoetida)

claims.reasoning: US customs restricts raw rice, lentils, fresh produce, meat, dairy, homemade foods.
claims.quote: fresh fruits, vegetables, raw rice, lentils, meat, dairy, or homemade foods

claims.reasoning: Carry 1-2 month supply for ongoing treatment.
claims.quote: Prescription Medicines (1-2 month supply if you are on ongoing treatment)

claims.reasoning: Common OTC meds recommended to carry from India.
claims.quote: paracetamol, ibuprofen, cough syrup, antacids

claims.weightNote: 158 cm linear, 23 kg (50 lb) per checked bag
claims.reasoning: Baseline airline constraint governing every bring-vs-buy tradeoff.
claims.quote: The total linear dimensions of each check-in bag should not exceed 158 cm (62 inches). The total weight must not be more than 23 kg (50 pounds).

claims.reasoning: Purchasable locally.
claims.quote: Stationery, you can buy that locally

claims.reasoning: IDP treats spices/plant products as items to avoid (customs). Contradicts common practice.
claims.quote: Spices or plant products

claims.reasoning: Bring prescription meds plus a first-aid kit.
claims.quote: Keep your prescription medicines, if any

claims.reasoning: Mittens/cap conditional on a colder destination.
claims.quote: if you are going to a colder country

claims.weightNote: wear heaviest layer on the flight
claims.reasoning: Pack only a light jacket; buy a thicker one on arrival.
claims.quote: you can get a thicker one once you land there.

claims.reasoning: Basic kitchenware from home helps for budget cooking.
claims.quote: getting some basic kitchenware from your home country will come in handy

claims.weightNote: 3-4 kg rice, 2 kg each lentil within 23 kg limit
claims.reasoning: Carry an initial food stock to bridge until you find Indian stores.
claims.quote: Check-in bags should not exceed 158 cm (62 inches) in total linear dimensions, with a maximum weight of 23 kg (50 pounds).

claims.reasoning: Maggi only sold at Indian grocery stores in the US.
claims.quote: you won't get Maggi in any other stores in the USA except the Indian ones.

claims.reasoning: Shoes, laptops, headphones much cheaper in US Black Friday sales.
claims.quote: They are very cheap abroad...Wait till the Black Friday sales.

claims.reasoning: Lightweight pressure cooker and basic utensils recommended.
claims.quote: Kitchen Supplies: Lightweight pressure cooker, frypan, spoons, plates, water bottle, vegetable chopping board, bowls, glasses, knives, tea strainer

claims.reasoning: Staples (rice, legumes, pickles, spices, tea, coffee) as essentials.
claims.quote: Food Items: Rice, legumes, pickles, spices, salt, sugar, snacks, tea leaves, coffee. Please carry only essential items you will require to avoid overpacking.

claims.reasoning: Common OTC + prescription meds and immunisation records.
claims.quote: Prescription medications; Cold, fever, cough, indigestion, toothache remedies; Anti-allergic medications; Antacids; Dettol; Bandages

claims.reasoning: Explicitly listed to avoid: heavy liquids, heavy books, expensive jewelry.
claims.quote: Items to avoid: Heavy liquid bottles; Heavy books and reading materials; Expensive jewelry

claims.reasoning: Bring winter jackets conditioned on destination weather.
claims.quote: Winter jackets and sweatshirts ... Check the overall weather of your study abroad destination to avoid overpacking.

claims.reasoning: Buy or borrow from library in the US.
claims.quote: purchase them from the host country or issue them from a library

claims.reasoning: Indian plugs may not fit; carry universal adapters + chargers.
claims.quote: Indian adapters might not work in the international switches; therefore, you must carry universal adapters

claims.reasoning: Bring spices, prescribed meds, and a pressure cooker in checked luggage.
claims.quote: essentials like a small quantity of Indian spices, prescribed medicines (with the doctor's prescription), and practical kitchen items such as a pressure cooker in your checked luggage

claims.reasoning: Bring thermal wear, heavy jackets, insulated boots for colder regions; bring a voltage converter (US 110V).
claims.quote: Winter gear such as thermal wear, heavy jackets, and insulated boots for colder regions.

claims.reasoning: Homemade/perishable food may be confiscated at customs.
claims.quote: Homemade or perishable food items, as they may be confiscated at customs

claims.reasoning: Jackets are cheap in the US when on sale; don't buy in India.
claims.quote: Don't buy winter wear in India...Jackets...are cheap when on sale

claims.reasoning: Rice/dhal waste luggage space and are available in the US.
claims.quote: Don't carry any rice or other dhal items

claims.reasoning: Bring as much masala/spice as possible.
claims.quote: Try to get as much as possible

claims.reasoning: One pressure cooker as essential Indian-cooking equipment.
claims.quote: Pressure Cooker: 1 (3 to 7 liters)

price.note: $225/pair in US even with eye insurance; a fraction of that in India

claims.reasoning: Eyeglasses very expensive in the US.
claims.quote: very expensive here. With an Eye Insurance, I paid $225 for one pair

price.note: 13 hangers for $1 at Walmart; Adidas shoes $18

claims.reasoning: Cosmetics, shampoo, hangers cheap in US.
claims.quote: Avoid any cosmetics product. They're cheap there ... 13 Hangers for $1 from Walmart

claims.reasoning: Formal coat/suit expensive in the US; bring from India.
claims.quote: formal coat/suit ... expensive in the US

price.note: 20 lb Ponni raw rice $26 in US Indian store (2010); Indian groceries ~4x India prices

claims.reasoning: Indian groceries marked up in US but rice is heavy and available; carry only dry low-water foods.
claims.quote: carry those things which have negligible water content. Dry foods are allowed usually.

price.note: iPhone 11 Pro 64GB: Rs 99,900 India vs $999 US

claims.reasoning: Smartphones, consoles, laptops, branded audio cheaper in US.
claims.quote: Iphone 11Pro 64GB in india vs USA: Rs.99,900 vs $999

claims.reasoning: Branded apparel/shoes cheaper in US, often with student discounts.
claims.quote: Sport shoes Nike Airmax, Nike Lunarstelos, New Balance

claims.reasoning: Rule of thumb: needs (food, everyday clothing) cheaper in India; wants/luxury/branded cheaper in US.
claims.quote: Items that fulfill needs - food, clothing and shelter - are almost always cheaper in India. Items that fulfill wants, or are luxury goods...are cheaper in the US

price.note: White rice/kg: Rs 61 ($0.64) India vs $4.58 US (~7x)

claims.reasoning: Rice ~7x cheaper in India, but weight limits constrain how much to carry.

price.note: Levi's 501: $22.71 India vs $53.35 US; Nike shoes $40.66 vs $92.58 (~2x)

claims.reasoning: Branded clothing ~2x more in US — supports bringing some from India, though branded US sales narrow the gap.
claims.quote: Jeans (Levi's 501): Rs 2,165.69 ($22.71) in India vs. Rs 5,087.40 ($53.35) in US

price.note: transport cost ≈ US purchase price; Instant Pot $60-80 on sale

claims.weightNote: red-flagged & inspected in checked bags; heavy/bulky
claims.reasoning: Cost of transporting a cooker roughly equals buying one in the US; not worthwhile unless in a remote area with no Indian store.
claims.quote: it's rather useless to take a cooker in flight. U can buy one after getting there. The over all cost u pay for taking that cooker ... is equal to the amount that costs for the cooker there

claims.reasoning: Indian 240V appliances won't run on US 120V without a transformer.
claims.quote: your 240v supply rice cooker won't work without a 240/120 transformer. And they occupy valuable luggage space

claims.reasoning: Original immigration docs + copies, immunization records, transcripts.
claims.quote: Original immigration documents ... Copies of all immigration documents (keep these separate from the originals) ... Immunization records and required forms ... Transcripts

claims.reasoning: UIUC advises clothing for a wide climate range (summer up to 100°F, winter below 0°F). Context only — takes no bring-vs-buy side.
claims.quote: In the summer, it can reach up to 100 degrees Fahrenheit. Winter temperatures can reach below 0 degrees Fahrenheit.

claims.reasoning: US restricts meats, fruits, vegetables, plants, soil, seeds; declare all food/ag to CBP.
claims.quote: meats, fruits, vegetables, plants, soil, seeds, and some plant-based handicrafts

claims.reasoning: Bring coat/warm socks/boots if you own them — may cost more in US.
claims.quote: pack a good coat, warm socks, and boots ... These items may be more expensive to buy in the U.S.

claims.reasoning: Dorm beds need XL twin sheets (~39x80"); blankets not provided, buy after arrival.
claims.quote: extra-long twin size bed, approximately, 39" x 80" ... Blankets will not be provided, but can be bought after you are here.

claims.reasoning: Bring hard-to-find snacks; carry $200-250 cash per person.
claims.quote: Snacks that you might have a hard time finding in the United States

claims.reasoning: Bulky household items — bedding, towels, dishes, utensils, fans, electronics — buy or ship in US.
claims.quote: shipped to the on-campus package center or an off-campus address

claims.reasoning: Bring season-appropriate clothing incl. full winter gear for Ohio winters.
claims.quote: Clothing appropriate for the season ... warm coat, gloves, scarf, hat, and winter shoes

claims.reasoning: Bring originals + 2-3 photocopies + scanned backups.
claims.quote: pack original documents, keep at least 2-3 photocopies and scan copies Google Drive/ hard drive.

claims.reasoning: Bring a personal medicine kit; personal-care items may not be easily accessible.
claims.quote: an adequate supply of personal care items, as they are perishable and may not be easily accessible.

claims.reasoning: Bring layerable winter clothing (1-2 waterproof jackets, thermals, beanies, gloves).
claims.quote: garments that can be layered to accommodate changing temperatures.

claims.reasoning: Bring spices, instant noodles, tea/coffee, chutneys if customs-permitted.
claims.quote: Ensure the food items you pack are allowed through U.S. customs.

claims.reasoning: Pack ~2-month starter of toiletries; body lotion important for winter.
claims.quote: a must for winter weather

claims.reasoning: Buy linens in US (bed size unknown); on-campus residents may bring twin XL sheets.
claims.quote: Buy bed linens in the USA since you don't know the exact size or type of bed ... if you plan to live in on-campus housing you can bring twin extra long bed sheets.

claims.reasoning: Small appliances (lamps, hair dryers) cheap in US; voltage/plug mismatch (110V). Bring only 110V adapters for laptop/phone.
claims.quote: Everyday electrical appliances such as desk lamps and hair dryers can be purchased cheaply in North America.

claims.reasoning: Bring favorite non-perishable foods; skip heavy books.
claims.quote: Bring your favorite non-perishable foods or snacks that will not be available or hard to find in North America.

claims.reasoning: Bring prescription meds (with doctor's signed list); buy OTC (aspirin) in US to save space.
claims.quote: Over the counter medications such as aspirin can be bought in North America to save space

claims.weightNote: wear heaviest jacket on flight; 1 checked bag up to 50 lb (23 kg)
claims.reasoning: Don't pack the heavy winter coat — buy on arrival; bring light layering items.
claims.quote: do not already own, upon arrival (heavy winter coat, summer clothes, etc.)

claims.reasoning: Pack minimal; buy toiletries/school supplies in US (orientation shopping trip).
claims.quote: don't bother packing large amounts of toiletries, school supplies, or other easily replaced items.

claims.weightNote: bulky; order online for delivery before arrival
claims.reasoning: No linens provided; order XL twin sheets online.
claims.quote: The cost to purchase sheets ranges from $15-$25 ... a pillow will cost about $10; towels cost about $15 ... Blankets can cost from $20-$75.

claims.reasoning: 220→110V mismatch; cheaper and easier to buy in US.
claims.quote: You'll most likely find it cheaper and easier to purchase these things here.

claims.reasoning: Carry supply + generic names; out-of-state (non-NY) prescriptions not accepted, so bring doctor's full description.
claims.quote: Prescriptions written outside New York State will not be accepted in the state, so your doctor should provide a complete description of any medication

price.note: gloves $10, wool hat $10, down jacket $100, boots $60-100

claims.reasoning: US winter-gear price benchmarks given; frames cost comparison as student's decision.
claims.quote: Gloves $10 Wool Hat $10 Down jacket $100 Boots $60-100
